patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 2/2] btrfs: test qgroup deletion races with squota writes

version_history.subject: [PATCH 2/2] btrfs: test qgroup deletion races with squota writes

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds four test cases to the Btrfs file system to cover various race conditions between qgroup deletion and squota writes. The tests aim to identify potential bugs in the handling of extent ownership references (EXTENT_OWNER_REF) when a subvolume is deleted or its qgroup is destroyed. The scenarios tested include freeing an extent whose owner qgroup is gone, creating an extent while the owning subvolume is being deleted, destroying a live subvolume's qgroup with delayed refs, and destroying a qgroup belonging to a still-mounted subvolume. The patch ensures that these operations are properly handled by the Btrfs kernel to prevent underflows or transaction aborts.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 1/2] btrfs: inline enable_quota helper in test 301

version_history.subject: [PATCH 1/2] btrfs: inline enable_quota helper in test 301

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch inlines the enable_quota helper function in test 301, removing unnecessary indirection and dead code. The helper is only used for simple quota enablement, which is always exercised by this test.

review_comments.author: Zorro Lang
review_comments.summary: Expressed neutrality towards the patch and suggested reviewing it separately from the second patch in the series. Did not provide any technical feedback or opinions on the patch itself.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Tue, May 12, 2026 at 05:43:33PM -0700, Boris Burkov wrote:
> The enable_quota helper in btrfs/301 takes a mode argument ('n', 's',
> or anything else) to switch between "no quota", "simple quota", and
> "full quota" enable.  Only the simple-quota path is ever exercised by
> this test, so the dispatch is dead weight and the indirection just
> obscures the actual btrfs command being run.
> 
> Inline the two call sites to call 'btrfs quota enable --simple'
> directly and drop the helper.
> 
> Signed-off-by: Boris Burkov <boris@bur.io>
> ---

Hahah, to be honest, the title '[PATCH 0/2] squota delete fstests' gave me
a minor heart attack. I thought it was a critical bug report about fstests
accidentally deleting itself while running :-D

>  tests/btrfs/301 | 14 ++------------
>  1 file changed, 2 insertions(+), 12 deletions(-)
> 
> diff --git a/tests/btrfs/301 b/tests/btrfs/301
> index 1f72a97b..31243aab 100755
> --- a/tests/btrfs/301
> +++ b/tests/btrfs/301
> @@ -169,16 +169,6 @@ do_enospc_falloc()
>  	do_falloc $file $sz
>  }
>  
> -enable_quota()
> -{
> -	local mode=$1
> -
> -	[ $mode == "n" ] && return
> -	arg=$([ $mode == "s" ] && echo "--simple")
> -
> -	$BTRFS_UTIL_PROG quota enable $arg $SCRATCH_MNT
> -}
> -
>  get_subvid()
>  {
>  	_btrfs_get_subvolid $SCRATCH_MNT subv
> @@ -198,7 +188,7 @@ prepare()
>  {
>  	_scratch_mkfs >> $seqres.full
>  	_scratch_mount
> -	enable_quota "s"
> +	$BTRFS_UTIL_PROG quota enable --simple $SCRATCH_MNT
>  	$BTRFS_UTIL_PROG subvolume create $subv >> $seqres.full
>  	local subvid=$(get_subvid)
>  	set_subvol_limit $subvid $limit
> @@ -421,7 +411,7 @@ enable_mature()
>  	# Sync before enabling squotas to reliably *not* count the writes
>  	# we did before enabling.
>  	sync
> -	enable_quota "s"
> +	$BTRFS_UTIL_PROG quota enable --simple $SCRATCH_MNT

This change doesn't seem related to patch 2/2. I think we can review
these 2 patches separately.

For this patch, I'm fine with or without it. If the btrfs list prefers
to have it, or move it to be a common helper, I'm good with that.

Thanks,
Zorro

>  	set_subvol_limit $subvid $limit
>  	_scratch_cycle_mount
>  	usage=$(get_subvol_usage $subvid)
> -- 
> 2.54.0
> 
>

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 0/2] squota delete fstests
patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series resurrects a previous attempt to land tests exercising edge cases of squota qgroup deletion, incorporating additional testing and feedback.

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH 3/7] btrfs: zoned: always set data_relocation_bg
Re: [PATCH 1/7] btrfs: zoned: document RECLAIM_ZONES flush state
Re: [PATCH 0/7] btrfs: fixes around generic/747 on zoned filesystems

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v6 5/7] locking: Add contended_release tracepoint to qspinlock

version_history.subject: [PATCH v6 5/7] locking: Add contended_release tracepoint to qspinlock

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds a contended_release tracepoint to the qspinlock implementation, allowing for tracing of lock contention and release events. The patch uses an arch-overridable queued_spin_release() function to ensure correct behavior across all architectures, including those with custom unlock implementations. When the tracepoint is disabled, only a single NOP instruction is added to the hot path, minimizing performance impact. When enabled, the contention check, trace call, and unlock are combined in an out-of-line function to further minimize hot path impact. The patch also includes estimates of binary size increases due to this change.

review_comments.author: Steven Rostedt
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer noted that the contended_release tracepoint is not properly disabled when the lock is already unlocked, and suggested adding a check to ensure the lock is locked before tracing.

The reviewer suggested removing a phrase from the patch description that is considered unnecessary in Git change logs.

Reviewer Steven Rostedt suggested removing a 'return' statement from the code, arguing that it is unnecessary and may cause bugs in the future if modified; instead of returning immediately, the function should be called directly

The reviewer, Steven Rostedt, pointed out a redundant call to queued_spin_release() in the code and requested its removal.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Tue,  5 May 2026 17:09:34 +0000
Dmitry Ilvokhin <d@ilvokhin.com> wrote:

---

Remove the ", introduced in the previous commit," That's useless in git
change logs.

---

Get rid of the "return;". What does it save you? It just makes it that you
need to duplicate the code. Even though it's a one liner, it can cause bugs
in the future if this changes. You could call the function:

  do_trace_queued_spin_release_traced(lock);

---

And then remove the duplicate call of "queued_spin_release()" here.

-- Steve

review_comments.author: Peter Zijlstra
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Peter Zijlstra pointed out that the patch adds unnecessary instructions to the hot path, specifically a CALL and JMP instruction in addition to the initial NOP, increasing binary size by 12-15 bytes. He also disagreed with the claim that UNINLINE_SPIN_UNLOCK=n configurations opt against binary size optimization on x86.
review_comments.raw_body:
This is not quite accurate. You add the (5byte) NOP for the static
branch, but then you also add another 5 bytes for the CALL and at least
another 2 bytes (possibly 5) for a JMP back into the previous stream.
That is 12-15 bytes added to what was a single MOV instruction.

That is quite ludicrous.

I disagree that UNINLINE_SPIN_UNLOCK=n opts against binary size. For x86
the unlock is smaller than a function call.


I really don't see how this is worth it.

review_comments.author: Dmitry Ilvokhin (author)
review_comments.summary:
The author addressed Peter's concern about the size increase of the generated code due to the added tracepoint. The author provided detailed assembly code comparisons between the baseline and patched versions, showing that while the total size increase is 9 bytes, the executed path only adds a 2-byte NOP instruction, which has special treatment in CPU.

Author acknowledges that the original binary size increase was due to a single 2-byte NOP instruction and asks if this change alleviates the reviewer's concerns.

Author acknowledged a characterization error in the patch description regarding UNINLINE_SPIN_UNLOCK on x86_64 and agreed to correct it
review_comments.raw_body:
Thanks for the feedback, Peter. This is exactly the kind of feedback I
was looking for.

I understand your concerns and initially I had exactly the same
thoughts, and after I looked into the generated code more carefully the
impact on the executed path is smaller than the total size increase
suggests.

Generated code of _raw_spin_unlock() for baseline (before the patch) is
31 bytes in total (x86_64, defconfig, GCC 11).

    3e0:  endbr64                          ; 4 bytes
    3e4:  movb $0x0,(%rdi)                 ; 3 bytes (unlock)
    3e7:  decl %gs:__preempt_count         ; 7 bytes
    3ee:  je   3f5                         ; 2 bytes
    3f0:  jmp  __x86_return_thunk          ; 5 bytes
    3f5:  call __SCT__preempt_schedule     ; 5 bytes
    3fa:  jmp  __x86_return_thunk          ; 5 bytes

Generated code of _raw_spin_unlock() with tracepoint (after the patch
applied) is 40 bytes in total.

    bc0:  endbr64                          ; 4 bytes
    bc4:  xchg %ax,%ax                     ; 2 bytes (NOP, static branch)
    bc6:  movb $0x0,(%rdi)                 ; 3 bytes (unlock)
    bc9:  decl %gs:__preempt_count         ; 7 bytes
    bd0:  je   bde                         ; 2 bytes
    bd2:  jmp  __x86_return_thunk          ; 5 bytes
    bd7:  call queued_spin_release_traced  ; 5 bytes
    bdc:  jmp  bc9                         ; 2 bytes
    bde:  call __SCT__preempt_schedule     ; 5 bytes
    be3:  jmp  __x86_return_thunk          ; 5 bytes

It is 40 bytes (+9 bytes compared to baseline, 2 bytes for NOP and 7
bytes for CALL and JMP).

But if we look at the executed path the picture is a bit different.

Baseline, in best case scenario of least number of executed
instructions.

    3e0:  endbr64                          ; 4 bytes (always executed)
    3e4:  movb $0x0,(%rdi)                 ; 3 bytes (unlock,
                                           ; always executed)
    3e7:  decl %gs:__preempt_count         ; 7 bytes (always executed)
    3ee:  je   3f5                         ; 2 bytes (always executed)
    3f0:  jmp  __x86_return_thunk          ; 5 bytes (executed if above
                                           ; je is not taken)
                                           ; rest is not executed
    3f5:  call __SCT__preempt_schedule     ; 5 bytes
    3fa:  jmp  __x86_return_thunk          ; 5 bytes

Tracepoint (again same case of least number of executed instructions).

    bc0:  endbr64                          ; 4 bytes (always executed)
    bc4:  xchg %ax,%ax                     ; 2 bytes (always executed, this is an
                                           ; only addition on the execution path).
    bc6:  movb $0x0,(%rdi)                 ; 3 bytes (unlock, always executed)
    bc9:  decl %gs:__preempt_count         ; 7 bytes (always executed)
    bd0:  je   bde                         ; 2 bytes (always executed)
    bd2:  jmp  __x86_return_thunk          ; 5 bytes (executed if above
                                           ; je is not taken)
                                           ; rest is not executed
    bd7:  call queued_spin_release_traced  ; 5 bytes
    bdc:  jmp  bc9                         ; 2 bytes
    bde:  call __SCT__preempt_schedule     ; 5 bytes
    be3:  jmp  __x86_return_thunk          ; 5 bytes

On the execution path we are getting 21 byte worth of instructions on
baseline against 23 bytes. The only addition on any executed path is the
2-byte NOP, that has a special treatment in CPU, cheap, but not entirely
free.

---

a single 2-byte NOP.

Does this change the picture for you, or is the NOP still a concern for
this path?

---

Fair point on the UNINLINE_SPIN_UNLOCK characterization, but
UNINLINE_SPIN_UNLOCKis always "y" on x86_64. The inlined case only
applies to s390 (unconditionally), csky and loongarch (when
!PREEMPTION). I'll remove this, thanks.

review_comments.author: Dmitry Ilvokhin (author)
review_comments.summary:
The author acknowledged a feedback about adding a comment to explain why the tracepoint is enabled only when contended_release is enabled, agreeing to make the change.

The author addressed a concern about the performance impact of tracing on x86_64, explaining that combining the trace and unlock in an out-of-line function minimizes hot path impact by avoiding callee-saved register save/restore.
review_comments.raw_body:
Thanks for the suggestion, will do here and in other places.

[...]

---

This is the scenario the comment above the static branch describes.
Here's what it looks like in practice on x86_64 (defconfig, compiled
with GCC 11).

Current design (trace + unlock combined, with return):
  
    endbr64
    xchg %ax,%ax                     ; NOP (static branch)
    movb $0x0,(%rdi)                 ; unlock
    decl %gs:__preempt_count
    je   preempt
    jmp  __x86_return_thunk
    call queued_spin_release_traced  ; cold
    jmp  preempt_handling            ; cold
    call __SCT__preempt_schedule
    jmp  __x86_return_thunk

With the trace-only function (no return, unlock after the call):
  
    endbr64
    push %rbx                        ; saves callee-saved rbx (!)
    mov  %rdi,%rbx                   ; preserve lock across call (!)
    xchg %ax,%ax                     ; NOP (static branch)
    movb $0x0,(%rbx)                 ; unlock
    decl %gs:__preempt_count
    je   preempt
    pop  %rbx                        ; callee-saved restore (!)
    jmp  __x86_return_thunk
    call queued_spin_release_traced  ; cold
    jmp  unlock                      ; cold
    call __SCT__preempt_schedule
    pop  %rbx
    jmp  __x86_return_thunk

Three extra instructions marked by "!" on the hot path (push, mov, pop),
all wasted when the tracepoint is off. That's the main reason for
combining trace and unlock in the same out-of-line function.

review_comments.author: Steven Rostedt
review_comments.summary:
The reviewer noted that the contended_release tracepoint is not properly disabled when the lock is already held, and requested a check to be added before calling queued_spin_release_traced()

Reviewer Steven Rostedt expressed concern over duplicated code in queued_spin_unlock() and suggested adding comments to explain its purpose, specifically mentioning the need for synchronization if changes are made to this location.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Thu, 14 May 2026 14:13:35 +0000
Dmitry Ilvokhin <d@ilvokhin.com> wrote:

---

Ah, because the return makes it into two tail calls.

I still don't like the duplication, perhaps add some more comments about
needing to update the other location if anything changes here? And perhaps
comment that this duplicate code helps the assembly.

-- Steve

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v6 4/7] locking: Factor out queued_spin_release()
patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch introduces a new function, queued_spin_release(), which is an arch-overridable unlock primitive. It refactors the existing arch-specific queued_spin_unlock() overrides on x86 (paravirt) and MIPS to use this new function. The change is preparatory for adding contended_release tracepoint instrumentation to queued_spin_unlock().

review_comments.author: Steven Rostedt
review_comments.summary: Requested a minor change to the changelog to avoid using 'next commit'.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Tue,  5 May 2026 17:09:33 +0000
Dmitry Ilvokhin <d@ilvokhin.com> wrote:

> Introduce queued_spin_release() as an arch-overridable unlock primitive,
> and make queued_spin_unlock() a generic wrapper around it.
> 
> This is a preparatory refactoring for the next commit, which adds
> contended_release tracepoint instrumentation to queued_spin_unlock().

In change logs, do not use "next commit". Instead say something like:

  In preparation for adding contended_release tracepoint instrumentation to
  queued_spin_unlock(), refactor the code to allow out of line calls when
  the tracepoint is enabled.

Or something like that.

-- Steve



> 
> Rename the existing arch-specific queued_spin_unlock() overrides on
> x86 (paravirt) and MIPS to queued_spin_release().
> 
> No functional change.
> 
> Signed-off-by: Dmitry Ilvokhin <d@ilvokhin.com>
> Reviewed-by: Paul E. McKenney <paulmck@kernel.org>

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v6 3/7] locking: Add contended_release tracepoint to sleepable locks

version_history.subject: [PATCH v6 3/7] locking: Add contended_release tracepoint to sleepable locks

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds a new tracepoint called contended_release to the Linux kernel's locking subsystem. The contended_release tracepoint fires when a lock is released under contention, allowing for correlation of lock hold time with waiter events by lock address. The patch modifies the slowpath unlock paths of various sleepable locks (mutex, rtmutex, semaphore, rwsem, percpu-rwsem, and RT-specific rwbase locks) to include calls to trace_contended_release(). This enables more detailed tracing and debugging of locking behavior in the kernel.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v6 6/7] locking: Factor out __queued_read_unlock()/__queued_write_unlock()
patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch refactors the __queued_read_unlock() and __queued_write_unlock() functions to be extracted from the queued_read_unlock() and queued_write_unlock() functions, respectively. This is a preparatory step for adding contended_release tracepoint instrumentation in a subsequent commit.

Same thing about using "next commit" in change logs.

-- Steve

>

> ---

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v6 7/7] locking: Add contended_release tracepoint to qrwlock

version_history.subject: [PATCH v6 7/7] locking: Add contended_release tracepoint to qrwlock

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series extends the contended_release tracepoint to queued rwlocks, using an out-of-line traced unlock approach similar to that used for queued spinlocks.

review_comments.author: Steven Rostedt
review_comments.summary: Raised concerns about duplicated code in the patch, suggesting that only the tracepoint should be added without duplicating existing locking logic.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v6 2/7] locking/percpu-rwsem: Extract __percpu_up_read()
patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch extracts the slowpath of percpu_up_read() into a new function __percpu_up_read(). The original percpu_up_read() is simplified, and the slowpath is moved to the new function. This change avoids increasing binary size due to the addition of a tracepoint in an inline function.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v6 1/7] tracing/lock: Remove unnecessary linux/sched.h include

version_history.subject: [PATCH v6 1/7] tracing/lock: Remove unnecessary linux/sched.h include

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch removes an unnecessary include statement from the lock tracing header file, as none of the trace events in this file reference anything from linux/sched.h.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v6 0/7] locking: contended_release tracepoint instrumentation
patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series adds a new tracepoint called contended_release that fires on the lock holder's side when a lock is released while there are waiters. This provides additional information such as hold time estimation and the holder's stack at release time, which can be used to measure how long a lock was held under contention. The patch also updates the behavior of reader/writer locks to fire the tracepoint for every reader releasing while a writer is waiting, not just the last reader.

review_comments.author: Steven Rostedt
review_comments.summary:
The reviewer noted that the contended_release tracepoint instrumentation may not be accurate for reader/writer locks, as it fires for every reader releasing while a writer is waiting, which could lead to spurious events.

The reviewer suggested rephrasing the patch description to use more descriptive language, specifically recommending against using 'next commit' and instead suggesting a more detailed explanation of the code refactoring.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Tue,  5 May 2026 17:09:33 +0000
Dmitry Ilvokhin <d@ilvokhin.com> wrote:

---

In change logs, do not use "next commit". Instead say something like:

  In preparation for adding contended_release tracepoint instrumentation to
  queued_spin_unlock(), refactor the code to allow out of line calls when
  the tracepoint is enabled.

Or something like that.

-- Steve

Reviewer Steven Rostedt suggested removing a 'return;' statement from the code, arguing that it is unnecessary and can cause bugs in the future if modified; instead of using the return statement, he recommended calling the function do_trace_queued_spin_release_traced(lock) directly

Reviewer Steven Rostedt requested removal of a duplicate call to queued_spin_release()
sentiment_signals: potential issue with reader/writer lock behavior

Dmitry Ilvokhin <d@ilvokhin.com> wrote:

change logs.

---

Get rid of the "return;". What does it save you? It just makes it that you

in the future if this changes. You could call the function:

  do_trace_queued_spin_release_traced(lock);

---

And then remove the duplicate call of "queued_spin_release()" here.

review_comments.author: Steven Rostedt
review_comments.summary:
The reviewer noted that the contended_release tracepoint instrumentation does not handle the case where a lock is released while it's being held by a task that has already been scheduled out, and requested the addition of a check to ensure that the lock is still held when the tracepoint fires.

Reviewer Steven Rostedt pointed out that the patch description should not use 'next commit' in the changelog, echoing a previous comment from another reviewer.

review_comments.author: Steven Rostedt
review_comments.summary:
The reviewer noted that the contended_release tracepoint instrumentation may not be thread-safe due to the use of a static branch check, and requested the patch author to consider using a different approach.

Reviewer Steven Rostedt simply echoed the original message 'ditto' without providing any additional context or feedback, indicating no clear signal.
review_comments.raw_body:

Dmitry Ilvokhin <d@ilvokhin.com> wrote:

review_comments.author: Peter Zijlstra
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Peter Zijlstra expressed uncertainty about the value of adding contended_release tracepoint instrumentation for qspinlock and rwlock, questioning whether it's worth the overhead.
review_comments.raw_body:
And for qspinlock.

I am really not sure this is worth the overhead for qspinlock / rwlock.

review_comments.author: Peter Zijlstra
review_comments.summary: Peter Zijlstra noted that the patch adds unnecessary instructions (12-15 bytes) to the contended_release tracepoint instrumentation, specifically a CALL and JMP instruction in addition to the original MOV instruction, which he considers 'quite ludicrous'. He also disagreed with the assumption that UNINLINE_SPIN_UNLOCK=n opts against binary size on x86, stating that the unlock is smaller than a function call.
review_comments.raw_body:
This is not quite accurate. You add the (5byte) NOP for the static
branch, but then you also add another 5 bytes for the CALL and at least
another 2 bytes (possibly 5) for a JMP back into the previous stream.
That is 12-15 bytes added to what was a single MOV instruction.

That is quite ludicrous.

I disagree that UNINLINE_SPIN_UNLOCK=n opts against binary size. For x86
the unlock is smaller than a function call.


I really don't see how this is worth it.

review_comments.author: Dmitry Ilvokhin (author)
review_comments.summary:
The author addressed Peter's concern about the size increase of generated code due to the patch, explaining that while the total size is larger, the executed path only adds a 2-byte NOP instruction, which has a special treatment in CPU and is not entirely free.

Author asks if a single 2-byte NOP is still a concern in this path after their previous changes

Author acknowledged a concern about the UNINLINE_SPIN_UNLOCK characterization being specific to certain architectures and agreed to remove it.
review_comments.raw_body:
Thanks for the feedback, Peter. This is exactly the kind of feedback I
was looking for.

I understand your concerns and initially I had exactly the same
thoughts, and after I looked into the generated code more carefully the
impact on the executed path is smaller than the total size increase
suggests.

Generated code of _raw_spin_unlock() for baseline (before the patch) is
31 bytes in total (x86_64, defconfig, GCC 11).

    3e0:  endbr64                          ; 4 bytes
    3e4:  movb $0x0,(%rdi)                 ; 3 bytes (unlock)
    3e7:  decl %gs:__preempt_count         ; 7 bytes
    3ee:  je   3f5                         ; 2 bytes
    3f0:  jmp  __x86_return_thunk          ; 5 bytes
    3f5:  call __SCT__preempt_schedule     ; 5 bytes
    3fa:  jmp  __x86_return_thunk          ; 5 bytes

Generated code of _raw_spin_unlock() with tracepoint (after the patch
applied) is 40 bytes in total.

    bc0:  endbr64                          ; 4 bytes
    bc4:  xchg %ax,%ax                     ; 2 bytes (NOP, static branch)
    bc6:  movb $0x0,(%rdi)                 ; 3 bytes (unlock)
    bc9:  decl %gs:__preempt_count         ; 7 bytes
    bd0:  je   bde                         ; 2 bytes
    bd2:  jmp  __x86_return_thunk          ; 5 bytes
    bd7:  call queued_spin_release_traced  ; 5 bytes
    bdc:  jmp  bc9                         ; 2 bytes
    bde:  call __SCT__preempt_schedule     ; 5 bytes
    be3:  jmp  __x86_return_thunk          ; 5 bytes

It is 40 bytes (+9 bytes compared to baseline, 2 bytes for NOP and 7
bytes for CALL and JMP).

But if we look at the executed path the picture is a bit different.

Baseline, in best case scenario of least number of executed
instructions.

    3e0:  endbr64                          ; 4 bytes (always executed)
    3e4:  movb $0x0,(%rdi)                 ; 3 bytes (unlock,
                                           ; always executed)
    3e7:  decl %gs:__preempt_count         ; 7 bytes (always executed)
    3ee:  je   3f5                         ; 2 bytes (always executed)
    3f0:  jmp  __x86_return_thunk          ; 5 bytes (executed if above
                                           ; je is not taken)
                                           ; rest is not executed
    3f5:  call __SCT__preempt_schedule     ; 5 bytes
    3fa:  jmp  __x86_return_thunk          ; 5 bytes

Tracepoint (again same case of least number of executed instructions).

    bc0:  endbr64                          ; 4 bytes (always executed)
    bc4:  xchg %ax,%ax                     ; 2 bytes (always executed, this is an
                                           ; only addition on the execution path).
    bc6:  movb $0x0,(%rdi)                 ; 3 bytes (unlock, always executed)
    bc9:  decl %gs:__preempt_count         ; 7 bytes (always executed)
    bd0:  je   bde                         ; 2 bytes (always executed)
    bd2:  jmp  __x86_return_thunk          ; 5 bytes (executed if above
                                           ; je is not taken)
                                           ; rest is not executed
    bd7:  call queued_spin_release_traced  ; 5 bytes
    bdc:  jmp  bc9                         ; 2 bytes
    bde:  call __SCT__preempt_schedule     ; 5 bytes
    be3:  jmp  __x86_return_thunk          ; 5 bytes

On the execution path we are getting 21 byte worth of instructions on
baseline against 23 bytes. The only addition on any executed path is the
2-byte NOP, that has a special treatment in CPU, cheap, but not entirely
free.

---

a single 2-byte NOP.

Does this change the picture for you, or is the NOP still a concern for
this path?

---

Fair point on the UNINLINE_SPIN_UNLOCK characterization, but
UNINLINE_SPIN_UNLOCKis always "y" on x86_64. The inlined case only
applies to s390 (unconditionally), csky and loongarch (when
!PREEMPTION). I'll remove this, thanks.

review_comments.author: Dmitry Ilvokhin (author)
review_comments.summary:
The author acknowledged a suggestion to add a comment explaining why the contended_release tracepoint fires for every reader releasing while a writer is waiting, not just the last reader.

The author addressed Steven Rostedt's concern about the performance impact of using a separate function for the contended_release tracepoint, explaining that three extra instructions on the hot path are wasted when the tracepoint is off and justifying combining the trace and unlock in the same out-of-line function.
review_comments.raw_body:
Thanks for the suggestion, will do here and in other places.

[...]

---

This is the scenario the comment above the static branch describes.
Here's what it looks like in practice on x86_64 (defconfig, compiled
with GCC 11).

Current design (trace + unlock combined, with return):
  
    endbr64
    xchg %ax,%ax                     ; NOP (static branch)
    movb $0x0,(%rdi)                 ; unlock
    decl %gs:__preempt_count
    je   preempt
    jmp  __x86_return_thunk
    call queued_spin_release_traced  ; cold
    jmp  preempt_handling            ; cold
    call __SCT__preempt_schedule
    jmp  __x86_return_thunk

With the trace-only function (no return, unlock after the call):
  
    endbr64
    push %rbx                        ; saves callee-saved rbx (!)
    mov  %rdi,%rbx                   ; preserve lock across call (!)
    xchg %ax,%ax                     ; NOP (static branch)
    movb $0x0,(%rbx)                 ; unlock
    decl %gs:__preempt_count
    je   preempt
    pop  %rbx                        ; callee-saved restore (!)
    jmp  __x86_return_thunk
    call queued_spin_release_traced  ; cold
    jmp  unlock                      ; cold
    call __SCT__preempt_schedule
    pop  %rbx
    jmp  __x86_return_thunk

Three extra instructions marked by "!" on the hot path (push, mov, pop),
all wasted when the tracepoint is off. That's the main reason for
combining trace and unlock in the same out-of-line function.

review_comments.author: Steven Rostedt
review_comments.summary:
reviewer noted that the contended_release tracepoint instrumentation may not work correctly for reader/writer locks, as it fires for every reader releasing while a writer is waiting, and requested clarification on this behavior

Reviewer Steven Rostedt expressed concerns about duplicated code in the patch, specifically mentioning that it appears in two tail calls and requested additional comments to ensure consistency if changes are made in one location.
sentiment_signals: potential issue with reader/writer lock behavior
review_comments.raw_body:
On Thu, 14 May 2026 14:13:35 +0000
Dmitry Ilvokhin <d@ilvokhin.com> wrote:

---

Ah, because the return makes it into two tail calls.

I still don't like the duplication, perhaps add some more comments about
needing to update the other location if anything changes here? And perhaps
comment that this duplicate code helps the assembly.

-- Steve

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH v6 5/7] locking: Add contended_release tracepoint to qspinlock

review_comments.author: Dmitry Ilvokhin

review_comments.author: Steven Rostedt
review_comments.summary: Agreed that the current design has duplication and suggested adding more comments to explain the need for updating other locations if changes are made here.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Thu, 14 May 2026 14:13:35 +0000
Dmitry Ilvokhin <d@ilvokhin.com> wrote:

> > > +void __lockfunc queued_spin_release_traced(struct qspinlock *lock)
> > > +{

> > > +	queued_spin_release(lock);  
> > 
> > And then remove the duplicate call of "queued_spin_release()" here.  
> 
> This is the scenario the comment above the static branch describes.
> Here's what it looks like in practice on x86_64 (defconfig, compiled
> with GCC 11).
> 
> Current design (trace + unlock combined, with return):
>   
>     endbr64
>     xchg %ax,%ax                     ; NOP (static branch)
>     movb $0x0,(%rdi)                 ; unlock
>     decl %gs:__preempt_count
>     je   preempt
>     jmp  __x86_return_thunk
>     call queued_spin_release_traced  ; cold
>     jmp  preempt_handling            ; cold
>     call __SCT__preempt_schedule
>     jmp  __x86_return_thunk
> 
> With the trace-only function (no return, unlock after the call):
>   
>     endbr64
>     push %rbx                        ; saves callee-saved rbx (!)
>     mov  %rdi,%rbx                   ; preserve lock across call (!)
>     xchg %ax,%ax                     ; NOP (static branch)
>     movb $0x0,(%rbx)                 ; unlock
>     decl %gs:__preempt_count
>     je   preempt
>     pop  %rbx                        ; callee-saved restore (!)
>     jmp  __x86_return_thunk
>     call queued_spin_release_traced  ; cold
>     jmp  unlock                      ; cold
>     call __SCT__preempt_schedule
>     pop  %rbx
>     jmp  __x86_return_thunk
> 
> Three extra instructions marked by "!" on the hot path (push, mov, pop),
> all wasted when the tracepoint is off. That's the main reason for
> combining trace and unlock in the same out-of-line function.

Ah, because the return makes it into two tail calls.

I still don't like the duplication, perhaps add some more comments about
needing to update the other location if anything changes here? And perhaps
comment that this duplicate code helps the assembly.

-- Steve

review_comments.author: Gregory Price
review_comments.summary: Raised a forward-looking question about propagating page flags through expand() instead of individually passing them. Suggested potential improvements in handling post_alloc_hook.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Thu, May 14, 2026 at 11:47:19AM -0400, Michael S. Tsirkin wrote:
> On Thu, May 14, 2026 at 11:41:45AM -0400, Gregory Price wrote:
> > On Thu, May 14, 2026 at 11:34:14AM -0400, Michael S. Tsirkin wrote:
> > > On Thu, May 14, 2026 at 11:32:26AM -0400, Gregory Price wrote:
> > > > > > 
> > > > > > Maybe mildly out of scope but worth asking:  Are there other flags that
> > > > > > should be retained/propogated on a split?  If so, rather than pass
> > > > > > was_reported, should we just take a temporary copy of the page flags and
> > > > > > pass them all in?
> > > > > > 
> > > > > > ~Gregory
> > > > > 
> > > > > 
> > > > > Not that I can see, no.
> > > > > 
> > > > 
> > > > You do this exact thing again later :]
> > > > 
> > > > https://lore.kernel.org/linux-mm/9a22e0f9bbe1278913754db6df76e291a006181a.1778616612.git.mst@redhat.com/
> > > > 
> > > > ~Gregory
> > > 
> > > I mean yes, reported and zeroed) Just zeroed does not exist at this
> > > stage in the series.
> > >
> > 
> > More of a forward looking question: if we already know we're adding
> > was_reported and was_zeroed, maybe we should just pass the page flags
> > through entirely and let expand() check them instead of passing it
> > through individually.
> > 
> > We end up doing something similar with post_alloc_hook later as well, so
> > I'm wondering if there's a general improvement that can be made here.
> > 
> > ~Gregory
> 
> sure, will do. do you want a bitwise thing for type safety? or just a
> long?
>

Can probably just do like:

flags = stable_page_flags(page);

plumbing...

expand(..., buddy_flags) {
	if (test_bit(PG_reported, buddy_flags))
		...
	if (test_bit(PG_zeroed, buddy_flags))
		...
}

or whatever makes sense.

~Gregory

review_comments.author: Gregory Price
review_comments.summary: Provided a possible implementation for propagating page flags using stable_page_flags() and test_bit().
review_comments.raw_body:
On Thu, May 14, 2026 at 02:00:31PM -0400, Michael S. Tsirkin wrote:
> On Thu, May 14, 2026 at 09:49:33AM -0400, Gregory Price wrote:
> 
> There are calls with no __GFP_ZERO but they do not allocate userspace pages.
> 
>   - drm_pagemap.c: GFP_HIGHUSER -- no zero. But this is a DRM device
>     page migration, the page content is preserved from the source.
> 
>   - test_hmm.c: GFP_HIGHUSER_MOVABLE -- no zero. Test driver, pages get
>     content from device.
> 
>   - mm/ksm.c: GFP_HIGHUSER_MOVABLE -- no zero. KSM merges identical
>     pages, content comes from the source page (copy).
> 
>   - mm/memory.c new_folio = GFP_HIGHUSER_MOVABLE
>     - no zero. This is CoW, content is copied from old page.
> 
>   - mm/userfaultfd.c: GFP_HIGHUSER_MOVABLE - no zero. Content comes from userspace via userfaultfd.
> 
>   - arm64/fault.c: __GFP_ZEROTAGS not __GFP_ZERO. MTE tag zeroing, not page zeroing. Page is zeroed separately.
> 

Right, so in all of these cases, it would be just as correct to pass
USER_ADDR_NONE I imagine :]

i.e. the user address is irrelevant, and the caller is responsible for
     sanitization before return if it's relevant.

Otherwise, passing (user_addr != -1) the buddy takes care of it for you.

Just an obvious security bonus to all of this, but by no means a
requirement for your set.  Just an observation.

> 
> I'd do this on top if possible.
>

Yeah reasonable.

~Gregory

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH] exportfs: drop unused is_export parameter from xtab_read() and xtab_write()
Re: [PATCH 00/38] lockd: Convert NLMv3 server-side procedures to xdrgen
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: This patch addresses a regression introduced in the conversion of NLMv3 server-side procedures to xdrgen, where the nlm4svc_lookup_file function was not properly zeroing the remainder of the file handle buffer after copying only the necessary bytes. This caused non-deterministic hash values for file handles shorter than 32 bytes, leading to lock state lookup failures. The patch adds a memset operation to ensure the entire buffer is zeroed before use.

review_comments.author: Chuck Lever
review_comments.summary: Reviewer noted that nlm4svc_lookup_file() introduced a regression by copying only fh.len bytes of the file handle without zeroing the remainder, leading to non-deterministic hash values and lock state lookup failures for handles shorter than 32 bytes. The reviewer suggested adding memset() to fix this issue.
review_comments.raw_body:
This appears to be a regression introduced by the (previous) NLMv4
series, and does not affect the current NLMv3 xdrgen conversion.

I can post a fix for this later today. Note that memset() was
removed as part of the xdrgen conversion because it zeroes much
more memory than actually needs to be initialized.


-- 
Chuck Lever

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledged that the new code introduced a bug by removing protections that made it safe to skip zeroing, agreed to fix it in a later patch.
review_comments.raw_body:
I couldn't quite tell. It almost sounded like the change in the
original patch was safe, but this series removed some protections that
made it safe to skip the zeroing? In any case it sounds like a real bug
that we should probably fix, though it can be done in a later patch.

The series itself seems mostly mechanical. You can add:

Reviewed-by: Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>

patches_acked.subject: Re: [PATCH 00/38] lockd: Convert NLMv3 server-side procedures to xdrgen

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH 4/4] iomap: fix out-of-bounds bitmap_set() with zero-length range
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: Fix for out-of-bounds bitmap_set() in iomap, addressing potential performance issues.

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong
review_comments.summary: Raised two specific technical concerns: (1) whether the Fixes tag is needed, and (2) whether ifs_set_range_uptodate() should return always true instead of ifs_is_fully_uptodate(folio, ifs).

review_comments.author: Zhang Yi
review_comments.summary: Acknowledged Joanne's concerns, agreed to fix the issue and return always true instead of ifs_is_fully_uptodate(folio, ifs).
review_comments.raw_body:
On 5/14/2026 11:08 PM, Joanne Koong wrote:
> On Wed, May 13, 2026 at 11:35\u202fPM Zhang Yi <yi.zhang@huaweicloud.com> wrote:
>>
>> From: Zhang Yi <yi.zhang@huawei.com>
>>
>> ifs_set_range_dirty() and ifs_set_range_uptodate() compute last_blk
>> as (off + len - 1) >> i_blkbits.  When off is 0 and len is 0, the
>> unsigned subtraction underflows to SIZE_MAX, producing a huge
>> last_blk and nr_blks value that causes bitmap_set() to write far
>> beyond the ifs->state allocation.
>>
>> Regarding ifs_set_range_uptodate(), it is temporarily safe because len
>> cannot be passed in as 0. However, for ifs_set_range_dirty() this is
>> reachable from __iomap_write_end(): when copy_folio_from_iter_atomic()
>> returns 0 (e.g. user buffer fault) and the folio is already uptodate,
>> the guard at the top of __iomap_write_end() does not trigger because
>> !folio_test_uptodate() is false, and iomap_set_range_dirty() is called
>> with copied == 0.
> 
> Is the Fixes: 4ce02c679722  ("iomap: Add per-block dirty state
> tracking to improve performance") tag needed for this?
> 
>>
>> Add a !len guard to both functions before the computation, so that a
>> zero-length range is a no-op.
>>
>> Signed-off-by: Zhang Yi <yi.zhang@huawei.com>
>> ---
>>  fs/iomap/buffered-io.c | 23 +++++++++++++++--------
>>  1 file changed, 15 insertions(+), 8 deletions(-)
>>
>> diff --git a/fs/iomap/buffered-io.c b/fs/iomap/buffered-io.c
>> index 27ab33edbdee..6fe5f7e998fd 100644
>> --- a/fs/iomap/buffered-io.c
>> +++ b/fs/iomap/buffered-io.c
>> @@ -67,11 +67,14 @@ static bool ifs_set_range_uptodate(struct folio *folio,
>>                 struct iomap_folio_state *ifs, size_t off, size_t len)
>>  {
>>         struct inode *inode = folio->mapping->host;
>> -       unsigned int first_blk = off >> inode->i_blkbits;
>> -       unsigned int last_blk = (off + len - 1) >> inode->i_blkbits;
>> -       unsigned int nr_blks = last_blk - first_blk + 1;
>> +       unsigned int first_blk, last_blk;
>>
>> -       bitmap_set(ifs->state, first_blk, nr_blks);
>> +       if (!len)
>> +               return true;
> 
> I think both callers of ifs_set_range_uptodate() use the return value
> to decide whether to mark the folio uptodate or not - does this still
> need to return ifs_is_fully_uptodate(folio, ifs) instead of always
> true?
> 

Yeah, you are right! I missed that, will fix.

Thanks,
Yi.

> Thanks,
> Joanne
> 
>> +
>> +       first_blk = off >> inode->i_blkbits;
>> +       last_blk = (off + len - 1) >> inode->i_blkbits;
>> +       bitmap_set(ifs->state, first_blk, last_blk - first_blk + 1);
>>         return ifs_is_fully_uptodate(folio, ifs);
>>  }
